div.head: Estimates for inverse power sums of zeroes
div.p: Now our objective is to state some estimates for the sum of inverse power of zeros. We start by constructing a partition of unity on D ∁ . Recall from Remark 3.1 that we assume D to be a disk of radius r 0 centered at the origin.
The functions ϕ, ϕ and the partition of unity (φ j ) j≥0 on D ∁ : Let

Let ϕ be a non-negative radial C ∞ c -function supported on [r 0 , x 3 r 0 ] such that ϕ = 1 on [x 1 r 0 , x 2 r 0 ] and ϕ(r 0 + yr 0 ) = 1 -ϕ(er 0 + yer 0 ), for 0 ≤ y ≤ e/2. Let ϕ be another non-negative radial C ∞ c function with the same support as ϕ, satisfying ϕ(r 0 + yr 0 ) = 1 for 0 ≤ y ≤ e/2 and ϕ = ϕ otherwise. Let φ 0 = ϕ and for j ≥ 1 let φ j (z) = ϕ(|z|/e j ). Then the collection of functions (φ j ) j≥0 is a partition of unity on D ∁ . Notation 6.2. For j ≥ 0, and for F either F α,n for some n ∈ N or F α,∞ , we define
div.formula: I F ; l ; j, j + 1 := F(ω)=0 φj (ω) =0 φ j (ω) ω l , I |•| F ; l ; j, j + 1 := F(ω)=0 φj (ω) =0 φ j (ω) |ω| l .
div.p: For j ≥ 1, and F same as above, let
div.formula: I F ; l ; 0, j := j-1 j ′ =0 I F ; l ; j ′ , j ′ + 1 , I |•| F ; l ; 0, j := j-1 j ′ =0 I F ; l ; j ′ , j ′ + 1 . Suppose F = F α,n for some n ∈ N. Since (φ j )
div.p: ∞ j=0 is a partition of unity, for sufficiently large j, I F ; l ; 0, j is the sum of inverse l'th power of all the zeroes of F α,n of which there are finitely many.

So we can define I F α,n ; l ; 0, ∞ as the limit of I F α,n ; l ; 0, j as j → ∞ (in a.s. sense). We cannot do this immediately for F = F α,∞ . To do this we derive some L 1 bounds. Proposition 6.1. Let Φ be a C ∞ c radial function supported on the annulus {z ∈ C : r 0 ≤ |z| ≤ x 3 r 0 }. Then, for R ≥ 1, l ∈ N, n ∈ N we have:
div.formula: E Φ z R 1 z l d[Z α,n ](z) ≤ C 1 (Φ) • l 2 • 1 r l 0 • 1 R l ,(40)
E Φ z R 1 |z| l d[Z α,n ](z) ≤ C 1 (Φ) • l 2 • 1 r l 0 • 1 R -l+⌊ 2 α ⌋ . (41
div.p: Here C 1 (Φ) > 0 is a constant depending on Φ. The same bounds also hold for Z α,∞ .
We prove Proposition 6.1 in Section 8.1.
div.formula: Proposition 6.2. For l ∈ N and n ∈ N E ϕ(z) z l d[Z α,n ](z) ≤ E ϕ(z) |z| l d[Z α,n ](z) ≤ C 2 • l 2 • 1 r l 0 ,
div.p: for some constant C 2 > 0. The same bounds also hold for Z α,∞ .
We prove Proposition 6.2 in Section 8.2.
Proposition 6.3 (Uniform bounds on sum of inverse powers). For l ≥ 1, and F either F α,n for some n ∈ N or F α,∞ , the following are true: (a) For all j ≥ 0 the following is well-defined
div.formula: I F ; l ; j, ∞ := ∞ j ′ =j I F ; l ; j ′ , j ′ + 1

(b) For all j ≥ 0

div.p: for some constant C 3 > 0.
(c) For all j ≥ 0

div.p: We prove Proposition 6.3 in Section 8.3.
Proposition 6.4 (Uniform bounds on sum of absolute value of inverse powers). Let
For l ≥ s α , and F either F α,n or F α,∞ , the following are true: (a) For all j ≥ 0 the following is well-defined

Let m be a positive integer. There exist positive constants K 2 (D, m), K 3 (D, m), K 4 (D, m), such that the following holds. Consider the ensemble Z α,n for some n ≥ m. There exists random variables (w r ) n r=0 such that the following hold:

Therefore, for all m ≤ l ≤ n σ n-l ω n n-l (n -l)! α/2 = (-1) (n-l) ξ n   (n-l)∧(rα-1) r=0 (-1) r w r • ξ l+r π α [l ; r] + η [n] l   . div.p: We prove Proposition 6.7 in Section 8.6.
7 The limiting procedure for α-GAF zeroes

In Sections 6 and 7 our objective is to lay down the steps of proving Theorem 3.2 using Theorem 2.1 with X ∞ = Z α,∞ and X n = Z α,n . In Section 6 we have obtained the necessary estimates for Z α,n . In this section we will lay down the steps to verify the conditions of Theorem 2.1, and obtain ( 12) from (8). The detailed proofs are in later sections.

div.head: Overview
div.formula: ν Φ,Ψ,D ( A ; Υ ) := π -1 (A) △ ζ 2 dL Σm,s (ζ) Σm,s △ ζ 2 dL Σm,s (ζ) ,(44)
div.p: where L Σm,s is the Lebesgue measure on Σ m,s . If we can establish the generalized Gibbs property, then we get that for all m ∈ N and all
div.formula: A ∈ A m in m 1 Z α,∞,out ν φ,ψ,D ( A ; Z α,∞,out ) ≤ P D|D ∁ [A|Z α,∞,out ] ≤ M 1 Z α,∞,out ν φ,ψ,D ( A ; Z α,∞,out ) ,
div.p: for some measurable functions m 1 , M 1 : P(D ∁ ) → (0, ∞). Then, by the Radon-Nikodym Theorem we will have
div.formula: m 2 Z α,∞,out △ ζ 2 ≤ dρ • ; Z α,∞,out dL (ζ) ≤ M 2 Z α,∞,out △ ζ 2 ,
div.p: for some measurable functions m 2 , M 2 : P(D ∁ ) → (0, ∞). Thus we get (11). Due to Theorem 2.1, it is enough to verify the conditions of approximate Gibbsianity for X n = Z α,n and X ∞ = Z α,∞ with respect to the probability kernel ν Φ,Ψ,D . As in the case of the Ginibre ensemble, here also we treat the Z α,∞,out configurations separately depending on m = N Z α,∞,out . Since the α-GAF zero ensemble is rigid up to order r α , the cases m = 0, . . . , r α -1 are trivial (Σ m,s is either the empty set or a singleton.) So we consider m ≥ r α . From now on we fix a value of m ≥ r α .

The three step procedure: We will verify the conditions of approximate Gibbsianity in three steps. ◮ In the first step we define the events (Ω(j)) j≥1 and also define, for each j ≥ 1, the sequence (n k ) ∞ k=1 and the sequence of events (Ω n k (j)) ∞ k=1 . We also verify that -Ω(j) ⊂ Ω(j + 1) for all j; ◮ In the third step we verify P Ω m ∞ \ Ω(j) → 0.
div.formula: -Ω(j) ⊂ Ω m ∞ ; -Ω(j) is measurable with respect to Z α,∞,out ; -Ω n k (j) is measurable with respect to Z α,n k ,out ; -Ω n k (j) ⊂ Ω m n k ; -Ω(j) ⊂ lim inf k→∞ Ω n k (j) a.
div.p: Parameters M , θ, δ: In each step we will define various events and prove relations between them. These events will involve three parameters M > 3, θ ∈ (0, 1), and δ ∈ (0, 1). The parameter M is to be thought of as large. The parameter θ is to be thought of as small. The parameter δ is also to be thought of as small. Our analysis will proceed by first fixing M and θ, and then taking δ sufficiently small.

Convention of naming events: We will name the events in the form Ω i:j n δ ; θ ; M . The subscript n indicates that the event is measurable with respect to the roots of F α,n . Here the subscript n can be either a positive integer or ∞. The superscript i : j indicates that the event is the j'th event with the subscript n defined in the i'th step. In step 3 we define the event Ω 3:2 ∞ • ; θ ; M which doesn't depend on the parameter δ. The sign • indicates that the parameter δ is not involved. δ 3 -negligible events and δ 3 -inclusion: Let us now introduce the notion of "δ 3 -negligible event" and "δ 3 -inclusion," where δ ∈ (0, 1). The reason for using δ 3 as opposed to just δ will be clear from Theorem 7.8. Definition 7.1 (δ 3 -negligible event). For δ ∈ (0, 1), we say an event E is δ 3 -negligible if there exists a constant C > 0 such that P(E) ≤ Cδ 3 .
The constant C can involve m and D.
Definition 7.2 (δ 3 -inclusion). For δ ∈ (0, 1) we say an event E 1 is δ 3 -included in an event E 2 if there exists a δ 3 -negligible event E 3 such that
We denote this relation by
div.formula: E 1 E 2 .

div.head: E3
div.p: If E 2 is obtained by excluding the δ 3 -negligible event E 3 from E 1 i.e., E 1 \ E 3 = E 2 , then we denote this relation by
div.formula: E 1 E 2 .

div.head: Outline of Step 1
div.p: In the first step our objectives are: to define the sequence of events (Ω(j))

∞ j=1 ; for each j ≥ 1 define the sequence (n k ) ∞ k=1 ; for each j and k define the event Ω n k (j); show the conditions on these events as stated in Theorem 13 are satisfied; show that Ω(j) ⊂ lim inf k→∞ Ω n k (j).
Step 1.1: In Definition 7.3 we introduce the event Ω 1:1 ∞ δ ; θ ; M . This event is measurable with respect to the roots of F α,∞ in the annulus
where we define R(δ) appropriately in Section 7.2.8. Recall that r 0 is the radius of D. Hence Ω 1:1 ∞ δ ; θ ; M is measurable with respect to the roots of F α,∞ outside D.

Step 1.2: In Definition 7.4 we define the event Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M . This event is measurable with respect to the roots of F α,n δ in the annulus An(δ), where n δ is defined in Section 7.2. Thus, this event is measurable with respect to the roots of F α,n δ outside D. The parameter n δ depends θ, but in the notation we suppress the dependence on θ since our analysis proceeds by first fixing M and θ and then taking δ sufficiently small.
Step 1.3: In Proposition 7.1 we show that for all M > 3, θ ∈ (0, 1) and for δ > 0 sufficiently small depending on M , the event
div.formula: Ω 1:1 ∞ δ ; θ ; M is δ 3 -included in the event Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M . So there is a δ 3 -negligible event E 1:1 δ such that Ω 1:1 ∞ δ ; θ ; M \ E 1:1 δ ⊂ Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M .
div.p: Note that, the event E 1:1 δ depends on θ. But as in the case of n δ , we refrain from writing it explicitly. Although the event E 1:1 δ doesn't depend on M , the relationship between the events as above holds for δ small enough depending on M .

Let (θ j ) ∞ j=1 be a sequence of positive real numbers which is monotonically decreasing and converges to 0. This is true because for each j and k the event Ω 1:1

div.head: Outline of Step 2
div.p: In this step our objective is to show that ( 13) holds for some (ϑ(j, k)) j≥1,k≥1 satisfying lim k→∞ ϑ(j, k) = 0 for all j ≥ 1.
Step 2.1: In Definition 7.5 we introduce an event Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M . This event is measurable with respect to the roots of F α,n δ outside D.
Step 2.2: In Proposition 7.3 we show that there exists a δ 3 -negligible event E 2:1 δ such that
div.formula: Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M \ E 2:1 δ ⊂ Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M . Thus Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M . E 2:1 δ
div.p: Both events Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M and Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M are measurable with respect to the roots of F α,n δ outside D. But the crucial difference between the events is that the event Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M involves the roots of F α,n δ in the annulus An(δ) (defined in (45)), whereas the event Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M involves all the roots of F α,n δ outside D.
Step 2.3: In Proposition 7.4 we define the event Ω 2:2 n δ δ ; θ ; M as a subset of the event Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M obtained by removing a δ 3 -negligible set E 2:2 δ :
div.formula: Ω 2:2 n δ δ ; θ ; M = Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M \ E 2:2 δ . Thus Ω 2:2 n δ δ ; θ ; M Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M . E 2:2 δ ⊃

• • • , k. That is, for complex numbers x 1 , • • • , x n , let s k := n j=1 x k j and e k := i1<•••<i k x i1 x i2 • • • x i k for 1 ≤ k ≤ n. Then e k = P k (s 1 , • • • , s k ) for 1 ≤ k ≤ n.

(ii) For a vector w = (w 1 , . . . , w N ) ∈ C N and for 1 ≤ k ≤ N , let w k denote the vector (w 1 , . . . , w k ).

We prove Proposition 7.11 in Section 11.3. Now, we show that P Ω m ∞ \ Ω(j) → 0 which is the end goal of Step 3. Theorem 7.12. lim j→∞ P Ω m ∞ \ Ω(j) → 0 . We prove Theorem 7.12 in Section 11.4. This concludes Step 3. This also concludes the description of the three step procedure of verifying the conditions of Theorem 2.1 in the context of the Z α,∞ ensemble. The rest of the article is devoted to proving the results we have stated thus far.
8 Proofs of the results in Section 6 8.1 Proof of Proposition 6.1
Proof of (40): We start with the identity (c.f. Section 2.4.1 in [34])
div.formula: 1 z l Φ z R d[Z α,n ](z) = 1 z l Φ z R ∆ log |F α,n (z)| dL(z) ,
div.p: where ∆ is the operator
div.formula: ∆ = 1 2π ∂ 2 ∂x 2 + ∂ 2 ∂y 2 . Since log E|F α,n (z)| 2 1/2
div.p: is a radial function and Laplacian of a radial function is also radial, we have

div.p: Then the above argument implies
div.formula: 1 z l Φ z R d[Z α,n ](z) = 1 z l Φ z R ∆ log F α,n (z) dL(z) .
div.p: Integrating by parts the right hand side we have
div.formula: 1 z l Φ z R d[Z α,n ](z) ≤ ∆ 1 z l Φ z R log | F α,n (z)| dL(z) . Therefore E 1 z l Φ z R d[Z α,n ](z) ≤ ∆ 1 z l Φ z R E log | F α,n (z)| dL(z) .

div.p: for some constant C 5 (Φ) > 0. This proves (40).
Proof of (41): Let K n be the covariance kernel of F α,n , that is
div.formula: K n (z, w) = n k=0 (zw) k (k!) α . Since Φ is a radial function on C, there exists a function Φ on R ≥0 such that Φ(z) = Φ(|z|). Therefore E 1 |z| l Φ z R d[Z α,n ](z) = C 7 1 r l Φ r R ∆ log(K n (r, r)) 1/2 r dr ,
div.p: for some constant C 7 > 0. Integrating by parts we get
div.formula: 1 r l Φ z R ∆ log(K n (r, r)) 1/2 r dr ≤ ∆ 1 r l Φ z R log(K n (r, r))
div.p: 1/2 r dr .
For r/R in the support of φ we have log(K n (r, r)) 1/2 ≤ log(K(r, r))
div.formula: 1/2 ≤ C 8 (α) r 2/α + |log r| ≤ C 9 (α) r 2/α 0 + |log r 0 | + C 10 R 2/α and ∆ 1 r l Φ r R ≤ C 11 (Φ) • l 2 • 1 R l+2 • 1 r l+2 0 . Therefore ∆ 1 r l Φ r R log(K n (z, z))r dr ≤ C 12 (Φ) • l 2 • 1 R l-2 α • 1 r l-2 α 0 .

div.head: Proof of Proposition 6.2

div.head: Proof of Proposition 6.6
div.p: Consider n, m, s, ω, ζ, ζ ′ as in the statement of this proposition. We start with some simple observations:

div.p: for all
div.formula: 1 ≤ i ≤ m , σ i ζ < m i r i 0 , and σ i ζ ′ < m i r i 0 .(60)
div.p: Using ( 58) and (59) we get for all 0 ≤ k ≤ n

div.formula: S n ; 0 ; (ζ ′ , ω) 2 2 = S n ; 0 ; (ζ , ω) 2 2 + m j=rα σ j ζ ′ -σ j ζ 2 • S n ; j ; ω , S n ; j ; ω + m j=rα 2ℜ σ j ζ ′ -σ j ζ • S n ; 0 ; (ζ , ω) , S n ; j ; ω + rα≤i<j≤m 2ℜ σ i ζ ′ -σ i ζ • σ j ζ ′ -σ j ζ • S n ; i ; ω • S n ; j ; ω .
div.p: Using triangle inequality we get
div.formula: D ζ , ω -A ζ , ζ ′ , ω ≤ D ζ ′ , ω ≤ D ζ , ω + A ζ , ζ ′ , ω ,(61)
div.p: where
div.formula: A ζ , ζ ′ , ω := m j=rα σ j ζ ′ -σ j ζ 2 • S n ; j ; ω , S n ; j ; ω + 2 m j=rα σ j ζ ′ -σ j ζ • S n ; 0 ; (ζ , ω) , S n ; j ; ω + 2 rα≤i<j≤m σ i ζ ′ -σ i ζ • σ j ζ ′ -σ j ζ • S n ; i ; ω , S n ; j ; ω .
div.p: Dividing throughout by D ζ , ω in (61) we get
div.formula: 1 -B ζ , ζ ′ , ω ≤ D ζ ′ , ω D ζ , ω ≤ 1 + B ζ , ζ ′ , ω ,(62)
div.p: where
div.formula: B ζ , ζ ′ , ω := m j=rα σ j ζ ′ -σ j ζ 2 • S n ; 0 ; ω , S n ; j ; ω S n ; 0 ; (ζ , ω)
σ i ζ ′ -σ i ζ • σ j ζ ′ -σ j ζ • S n ; i ; ω , S n ; j ; ω S n ; 0 ; (ζ , ω)2 2 .
div.p: Using (58) we get
div.formula: S n ; 0 ; (ζ , ω) , S n ; j ; ω = n k=0 σ k (ζ , ω)σ k-j (ω) n k k! α = n k=0 m i=0 σ i (ζ)σ k-i (ω)σ k-j (ω) n k k! α = m i=0 σ i (ζ) n k=0 σ k-i (ω)σ k-j (ω) n k k! α ≤ m i=0 σ i (ζ) • S n ; i ; (ω) , S n ; j ; ω ,
div.p: and thus
div.formula: m j=rα σ j ζ ′ -σ j ζ • S n ; 0 ; (ζ , ω) , S n ; j ; ω S n ; 0 ; (ζ , ω) 2 2 ≤ m i=0 m j=rα σ j ζ ′ -σ j ζ • σ i ζ • S n ; i ; ω , S n ; j ; ω S n ; 0 ; (ζ , ω)2 2
div.p: Combining ( 63), (64) and using ( 43) and (60) we get
div.formula: B ζ , ζ ′ , ω ≤ K 6 (D, m) • D ζ , ω
div.p: for some K 6 (D, m). Therefore, from (62) we get
div.formula: 1 -K 6 (D, m) • D ζ , ω ≤ D ζ ′ , ω D ζ , ω ≤ 1 + K 6 (D, m) • D ζ , ω .
div.p: This concludes the proof of Proposition 6.6.
8.6 Proof of Proposition 6.7
Suppose the event Ω m n happens. Let ζ be a vector consisting of the roots of F α,n inside D. Fix 1 ≤ k ≤ n -m. Then we have
div.formula: σ k ζ , ω = min{k,m} r=0 σ r ζ σ k-r ω .

div.head: It follows that
div.formula: σ k ω = σ k ζ , ω - min{k,m} r=1 σ r ζ σ k-r ω .
div.p: We can similarly expand each of the lower order term σ k-r ω in terms of σ j ζ , ω and obtain an expansion of σ k ω in terms of σ j ζ , ω , j = 1, . . . , k. In this way we get
div.formula: σ k ω = σ k ζ , ω + k r=1 w r σ k-r ζ , ω .(65)
div.p: The coefficient of σ k ζ , ω is 1. The rest of the coefficients are polynomials in σ j ζ , j = 1, . . . , m. They satisfy the recurrence relation
where
div.formula: σ n-l ω n n-l (n -l)! α/2 = n-l r=0 (-1) n-l-r • w r • ξ l+r ξ n • 1 π α [l ; r]
div.p: with w 0 := 1.
Using π α [l ; r] ≥ l rα/2 and the fact that E|ξ| 2 = 1 if ξ is a complex Gaussian random variable, we get
div.formula: E η [n] l 2 ½ Ω m n 1/2 ≤ n-l r=rα (K 7 (D, m)) r l rα/2 ≤ K 8 (D, m) rα l rαα/2
div.p: for l ≥ K 9 (D, m). This concludes the proof of Proposition 6.7.
9 Proofs of the results in Step 1 of Section 7
9.1 Some auxiliary lemmas Lemma 9.1. Let F be either F α,∞ or F α,n for some n ∈ N. Then, except on a δ 3 -negligible event, we have:
div.formula: max 1≤l≤C(δ) I F ; l ; 0, ∞ -I F ; l ; 0, k(δ) ≤ δ ∧ ǫ(δ) ; I |•| F ; s α ; 0, ∞ -I |•| F ; s α ; 0, k(δ) ≤ δ ∧ ǫ(δ) .
div.p: Proof. From Propositions 6.3 and condition (i) defining k(δ) we have 1≤l≤C(δ)
div.formula: P I F ; l ; k(δ), ∞ > C 3 • l 2 • 1 r l 0 • exp -k(δ) l 2 ≤ 1≤l≤C(δ) exp -k(δ) l 2 ≤ δ 3 .
div.p: Therefore, using condition (ii) defining k(δ), we get that except on a δ 3 -negligible event max 1≤l≤C(δ)
div.formula: I F ; l ; 0, ∞ -I F ; l ; 0, k(δ) ≤ max 1≤l≤C(δ) C 3 • l 2 • 1 r l 0 • exp -k(δ) l 2 ≤ δ ∧ ǫ(δ) .
div.p: Using Propositions 6.4 and condition (i) defining k(δ), we have
div.formula: P I |•| F ; s α ; k(δ), ∞ > C 4 • s 2 α • 1 r sα 0 • exp -k(δ) s α 2 ≤ exp -k(δ) s α 2 ≤ δ 3 .
div.p: Therefore, using condition (ii) defining k(δ), we get that except on a δ 3 -negligible event
div.formula: I |•| F ; s α ; 0, ∞ -I |•| F ; s α ; 0, k(δ) ≤ C 4 • s 2 α • 1 r sα 0 • exp -k(δ) s α 2 ≤ δ ∧ ǫ(δ) .
div.p: This concludes the proof of Lemma 9.1.
Lemma 9.2. Consider θ ∈ (0, 1) and δ ∈ (0, 1). Except on a δ 3 -negligible event we have:
div.formula: max 0≤i≤m max rα≤j≤m Q δ i,j Z ′ [δ] -Q δ i,j Z ′′ [δ] < δ ; Q δ,avg tail Z ′ [δ] -Q δ,avg tail Z ′′ [δ] < δ .
div.p: Proof. Condition (i) defining g(δ) implies that except on a δ 3 -negligible event we have max 1≤l≤C(δ)
I F α,n δ ; l ; 0, k(δ) ≤ g(δ) -1 .
By Lemma 9.1 we have that except on a δ 3 -negligible event max 1≤l≤C(δ)
I F α,n δ ; l ; 0, ∞ -I F α,n δ ; l ; 0, k(δ) < ǫ(δ) .
Therefore, using condition (i) defining ǫ(δ) we get that except on a δ 3 -negligible event
div.formula: max 0≤i≤m max rα≤j≤m Q δ i,j Z ′ [δ] -Q δ i,j Z ′′ [δ] < δ .
div.p: Similarly, from condition (ii) defining ǫ(δ) we get that except on a δ 3 -negligible event
div.formula: Q δ,avg tail Z ′ [δ] -Q δ,avg tail Z ′′ [δ] < δ .
div.p: This concludes the proof of Lemma 9.2.
Lemma 9.3. Consider θ ∈ (0, 1) and δ ∈ (0, 1). Except on a δ 3 -negligible we have:
div.formula: max 1≤l≤C(δ) I F α,∞ ; l ; 0, k(δ) -I F α,n δ ; l ; 0, k(δ) ≤ δ ∧ ǫ(δ) ;(67)
div.p: Proof. From condition (i) defining n δ we have except on a δ 3 -negligible event
div.formula: max 1≤l≤C(δ) max 0≤k≤k(δ) I F α,∞ ; l ; k, k + 1 -I F α,n δ ; l ; k, k + 1 ≤ δ ∧ ǫ(δ) k(δ) + 1 .
div.p: Using triangle inequality we get (67). From condition (i) defining n δ we have except on a δ 3 -negligible event max 0≤k≤k(δ)
div.formula: I |•| F α,∞ ; s α ; k, k + 1 -I |•| F α,n δ ; s α ; k, k + 1 ≤ δ ∧ ǫ(δ) k(δ) + 1 .
div.p: Using triangle inequality we get (68).
Lemma 9.4. Consider θ ∈ (0, 1) and δ ∈ (0, 1). Except on a δ 3 -negligible event we have:
div.formula: max 0≤i≤m max rα≤j≤m Q δ i,j Z[δ] -Q δ i,j Z ′ [δ] < δ ; Q δ,avg tail Z[δ] -Q δ,avg tail Z ′ [δ] < δ .
div.p: Proof. Condition (i) defining g(δ) implies that except on a δ 3 -negligible event we have
div.formula: max 1≤l≤C(δ) I F α,n δ ; l ; 0, k(δ) ≤ g(δ) -1 , max 1≤l≤C(δ) I F α,∞ ; l ; 0, k(δ) ≤ g(δ) -1 .
div.p: From Lemma 9.3 we have that except on a δ 3 -negligible event
div.formula: max 1≤l≤C(δ) I F α,∞ ; l ; 0, k(δ) -I F α,n δ ; l ; 0, k(δ) < ǫ(δ) .
div.p: Therefore, from condition (i) defining ǫ(δ) we get that except on a δ 3 -negligible event
div.formula: max 0≤i≤m max rα≤j≤m Q δ i,j Z[δ] -Q δ i,j Z ′ [δ] < δ .
div.p: Similarly, from condition (ii) defining ǫ(δ) we get that except on a δ 3 -negligible event
div.formula: Q δ,avg tail Z[δ] -Q δ,avg tail Z ′ [δ] < δ .
div.p: This concludes the proof of Lemma 9.4.

div.head: Proof of Theorem 7.2
div.p: Recall from ( 47) and (48) that
div.formula: Ω(j) = lim inf k→∞ Ω 1:1 ∞ δ k ; θ j ; M j and Ω n k (j) = Ω 1:1 n δ k δ k ; θ j ; M j .
div.p: From Proposition 7.1 we get
div.formula: Ω 1:1 ∞ δ k ; θ ; M j \ E 1:1 δ k ⊂ Ω 1:1 n δ k δ k ; θ ; M j ,
div.p: and
div.formula: P E 1:1 δ k < C 1:1 δ 3 k . Using (46) we get k P E 1:1 δ k < ∞ .
div.p: Therefore, using the first Borel-Cantelli lemma we get
div.formula: Ω(j) ⊂ lim inf k→∞ Ω n k (j) a.s.
div.p: This concludes the proof of Theorem 7.2.

10 Proofs of the results in Step 2 of Section 7 10.1 Some auxiliary lemmas Lemma 10.1. Suppose the event Ω m n δ occurs. Let ζ be a vector consisting of the roots of F α,n δ inside D. Let ω be a vector consisting of the roots of F α,n δ outside D. Then we have:
div.formula: σ k ζ , ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (n δ -k)! α = |ξ n δ -k | 2 Π F α,n δ ; in 2 |ξ 0 | 2 for 0 ≤ k ≤ n δ ;(69)

S n δ ; 0 ; (ζ, ω) 2 2 = σ n δ -m ω 2 (n δ !) α n δ k=0 σ k ζ , ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (n δ -k)! α ; (70) S n δ ; 0 ; (ζ, ω) 2 2 = 1 |ξ n δ | 2 n δ l=0 |ξ l | 2 ; (71) n δ k=0 σ k ζ , ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (n δ -k)! α = Π F α,n δ ; in 2 |ξ 0 | 2 n δ k=0 |ξ k | 2 . (72
div.p: Proof. From the relation between the roots and the coefficients of the polynomial F α,n δ we have
div.formula: σ k ζ , ω n δ k k! α/2 = (-1) k ξ n δ -k ξ n δ (73) for all 0 ≤ k ≤ n δ . Therefore for all 0 ≤ k ≤ n δ 1 n δ k k! α σ k ζ , ω σ n δ ζ , ω 2 = 1 (n δ !) α |ξ n δ -k | 2 |ξ 0 | 2 .
div.p: Multiplying both sides by Π F α,n δ ; in 2 and using
div.formula: Π F α,n δ ; in σ n δ ζ , ω = 1 σ n δ -m ω
div.p: we get (69). The equality in (70) follows simply by expanding the norm:
div.formula: S n δ ; 0 ; (ζ, ω) 2 2 = n δ k=0 σ k ζ , ω n δ k k! α/2 2 = σ n δ -m ω 2 (n δ !) α n δ k=0 σ k ζ , ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (n δ -k)! α .
div.p: Similarly, expanding the norm and and using (73) we get
div.formula: S n δ ; 0 ; (ζ, ω) 2 2 = n δ k=0 σ k ζ , ω n δ k k! α/2 2 = 1 |ξ n δ | 2 n δ l=0 |ξ l | 2 .
div.p: Equation (72) follows by taking sum over k in (69):
div.formula: n δ k=0 σ k ζ , ω σ n δ -m ω 2 ((n δ -k)!) α = Π F α,n δ ; in 2 |ξ 0 | 2 n δ k=0 |ξ k | 2 .
div.p: This concludes the proof of Lemma 10.1.
Lemma 10.2. On the event Ω m n δ we have
div.formula: S n δ ; i ; ω ⊙ ½ [n δ -C(δ),n δ ] , S n δ ; j ; ω ⊙ ½ [n δ -C(δ),n δ ] = σ n δ -m ω 2 (n δ !) α Q δ i,j Z ′′ [δ]
div.p: for all 0 ≤ i ≤ m and r α ≤ j ≤ m, where ω is a vector consisting of the roots of F α,n δ outside D. (See Notation 7.3 for explanation of the term in the l.h.s. of the above equation.)
Proof. Observe that for 0
div.formula: ≤ i ≤ m and i ≤ k ≤ n δ -m σ k-i ω σ n δ -m ω = P q I F α,n δ ; 1 ; 0, ∞ , • • • , I F α,n δ ; q ; 0, ∞ = Z ′′ q [δ] , with q = (n δ -m) -(k -i). Therefore, for 0 ≤ i ≤ m and r α ≤ j ≤ m we have S n δ ; i ; ω ⊙ ½ [n δ -C(δ),n δ ] , S n δ ; j ; ω ⊙ ½ [n δ -C(δ),n δ ] = n δ k=n δ -C(δ) σ k-i ω n δ k k! α/2 σ k-j ω n δ k k! α/2 = σ n δ -m ω 2 (n δ !) α n δ k=n δ -C(δ) σ k-i ω σ n δ -m ω σ k-j ω σ n δ -m ω (n δ -k)! α = σ n δ -m ω 2 (n δ !) α Q δ i,j Z ′′ [δ] ,
div.p: (using ( 53)) .
This concludes the proof of Lemma 10.2.

div.head: Proof of Proposition 7.3
div.p: Consider M > 3 and θ ∈ (0, 1). Our objective is to verify that for δ ∈ (0, 1) sufficiently small depending on M , the conditions defining Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M hold on the event Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M except on a δ 3 -negligible event.
(i) Condition (i) defining Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M is same as condition (i) defining Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M .
(ii) From condition (ii) defining Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M we have
div.formula: max 1≤s<sα I F α,n δ ; s ; 0, k(δ) ≤ M + 1 .
div.p: Using Lemma 9.1, sacrificing a δ 3 -negligible event, we get
div.formula: max 1≤s<sα I F α,n δ ; s ; 0, ∞ ≤ M + 1 + δ ≤ M + 2 ,
div.p: which is condition (ii) defining Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M .
(iii) By condition (iii) defining Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M + 1 we have
div.formula: I |•| F α,n δ ; s α ; 0, k(δ) ≤ M + 1 .
div.p: Using Lemma 9.1, sacrificing a δ 3 -negligible event, we get
which is condition (iii) defining Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M .
(iv) By condition (iv) defining Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M + 1 we have
div.formula: max 0≤i≤m max rα≤j≤m Q δ i,j Z ′ [δ] ≤ M + 1 .
div.p: Using Lemma 9.2, sacrificing a δ 3 -negligible event, we get
div.formula: max 0≤i≤m max rα≤j≤m Q δ i,j Z ′′ [δ] ≤ M + 1 + δ ≤ M + 2 ,
div.p: which is condition (iv) defining Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M .
(v) By condition (v) defining Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M + 1 we have
div.formula: (M + 1) -1 ≤ Q δ,avg tail Z ′ [δ] ≤ M + 1 .
div.p: Using Lemma 9.2, taking δ < (M + 1) -1 -(M + 2) -1 and sacrificing a δ 3 -negligible event, we get
div.formula: (M + 2) -1 ≤ Q δ,avg tail Z ′′ [δ] ≤ M + 2 ,
div.p: which is condition (v) defining Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M . This concludes the proof of Proposition 7.3.

div.head: Proof of Proposition 7.4
div.p: Consider M > 3, θ ∈ (0, 1), and δ ∈ (0, 1). Suppose the event Ω m n δ occurs. Let ζ be a vector consisting of the roots of F α,n δ inside D. Let ω be a vector consisting of the roots of F α,n δ outside D. From the relationship between the roots and the coefficients of the polynomial F α,n δ we get
div.formula: σ k ζ , ω n δ k k! α/2 = (-1) k ξ n δ -k ξ n δ
div.p: for all 0 ≤ k ≤ n δ . By Proposition 6.7 we have for 0
div.formula: ≤ k ≤ n δ -m σ k ω n δ k k! α/2 = k r=0 (-1) k-r • w r • ξ l+r π α [l ; r] . Therefore, for 0 ≤ k ≤ n δ -m we have σ k ω σ k ζ , ω = 1 + k r=1 (-1) k-r • w r • ξ n δ -k+r ξ n δ -k • 1 π α [n δ -k ; r] .
div.p: Thus, our objective is to show that except on a δ 3 -negligible event we have
div.formula: n δ -l r=1 (-1) n δ -l-r • w r • ξ l+r ξ l • 1 π α [l ; r] ≤ 1 2
div.p: for all l in the range L(δ) ≤ l ≤ L(δ) + h(L(δ)). By the inequality 1 + x ≥ √ x we have
div.formula: 1 π α [l ; r] ≤ 1 l rα/4 (r!) α/4 . By Proposition 6.7 we have |w r | ≤ (K 2 (D, m)) r . Therefore, for L(δ) ≤ l ≤ L(δ) + h(L(δ)) n δ -l r=1 (-1) n δ -l-r • w r • ξ l+r ξ l • 1 π α [l ; r] ≤ n δ -l r=1 (K 2 (D, m)) r l rα/4 (r!) α/4
div.p: ξ l+r ξ l .
Therefore, assuming δ is small enough so that L(δ) α/8 > K 2 (D, m), it is enough to show that
div.formula: P n δ -l r=1 1 l rα/8 (r!) α/4 ξ l+r ξ l ≤ 1 2 for all L(δ) ≤ l ≤ L(δ) + h(L(δ)) ≤ δ 3 .(74)
div.p: Using the fact that if ξ is complex Gaussian then |ξ| 2 is an exponential random variable with mean 1, we can deduce that
div.formula: P ξ l+r ξ l > x ≤ 1 x 2 , for 1 ≤ r ≤ n δ -l and L(δ) ≤ l ≤ L(δ) + h(L(δ)). Therefore P ξ l+r ξ l > l rα/16 (r!) α/8 ≤ 1 l rα/8 (r!) α/4 .(75)
div.p: For L(δ) ≤ l ≤ L(δ) + h(L(δ)) let E(l) be the event that for all r ≥ 1 ξ l+r ξ l ≤ l rα/16 (r!) α/8 .
On this event, assuming δ-is small enough so that L(δ) -α/16 < 1/3, we have
div.formula: n δ -l r=1 1 l rα/8 (r!) α/4 ξ l+r ξ l ≤ ∞ r=1 1 l rα/16 (r!) α/8 ≤ ∞ r=1 1 l rα/16 ≤ 1 2
div.p: For each l in the range L(δ) ≤ l ≤ L(δ) + h(L(δ)) using (75) we get
div.formula: P(E(l) c ) = P ξ l+r ξ l > l rα/16 (r!) α/8 for some r ≤ ∞ r=1 1 l rα/8 (r!) α/4 ≤ C 0 l α/8 ,
div.p: where C 0 is defined in (49). By a union over l and using condition (i) defining L(δ) we get
div.formula: P n δ -l r=1 1 l rα/8 (r!) α/4 ξ l+r ξ l > 1 2 for some L(δ) ≤ l ≤ L(δ) + h(L(δ)) ≤ L(δ)+h(L(δ)) l=L(δ) P(E(l) c ) ≤ L(δ)+h(L(δ)) l=L(δ) C 0 l α/8 ≤ δ 3 .
div.p: Thus we get (74). This concludes the proof of Proposition 7.4.

div.head: Proof of Proposition 7.5
div.p: Consider M > 3, θ ∈ (0, 1), and δ ∈ (0, 1). Suppose the event Ω m n δ occurs. Let ζ be a vector consisting of the roots of F α,n δ inside D. Let ω be a vector consisting of the roots of F α,n δ outside D. By Proposition 7.4, we have, except on a δ 3 negligible event,
div.formula: 2 3 σ k ω ≤ σ k ζ , ω ≤ 2 σ k ω , for n δ -L(δ) -h(L(δ)) ≤ k ≤ n δ -L(δ). Changing the variable from k to l = n δ -k we get 4 9 • 1 h(L(δ)) L(δ)+h(L(δ)) l=L(δ) σ n δ -l ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (l!) α ≤ 1 h(L(δ)) L(δ)+h(L(δ)) l=L(δ) σ n δ -l ζ , ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (l!) α ≤ 4 • 1 h(L(δ)) L(δ)+h(L(δ)) l=L(δ) σ n δ -l ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (l!) α .
div.p: We can rewrite the term in the middle of the inequality above, using (69) from Lemma 10.1, as
div.formula: 1 h(L(δ)) L(δ)+h(L(δ)) l=L(δ) σ n δ -l ζ , ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (l!) α = Π F α,n δ ; in 2 |ξ 0 | 2 1 h(L(δ)) L(δ)+h(L(δ)) l=L(δ) |ξ l | 2 .
div.p: By condition (ii) defining L(δ), we have, except on a δ 3 -negligible event,
div.formula: 1 2 < 1 h(L(δ)) L(δ)+h(L(δ)) l=L(δ) |ξ l | 2 < 3 2 . Hence 8 27 • 1 h(L(δ)) L(δ)+h(L(δ)) l=L(δ) σ n δ -l ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (l!) α ≤ Π F α,n δ ; in 2 |ξ 0 | 2 ≤ 8 • 1 h(L(δ)) L(δ)+h(L(δ)) l=L(δ) σ n δ -l ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (l!) α .
div.p: From here, using (52) we get (54). Now suppose the event Ω 2:2 n δ δ ; θ ; M occurs. We want to establish (55). From Proposition 7.4 we have Ω 2:2 n δ δ ; θ ; M is a subset of Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M , and from condition (v) defining Ω 2:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M we have
div.formula: (M + 2) -1 ≤ Q δ,avg tail Z ′′ [δ] ≤ M + 2 . So, (54) implies Π F α,n δ ; in 2 |ξ 0 | 2 ≥ 8 27 • 1 M + 2
div.p: By condition (ii) defining n δ we have
div.formula: n δ k=0 |ξ k | 2 > 1 2 n δ
div.p: except on a δ 3 -negligible event. Therefore, using (72) from Lemma 10.1, we get
div.formula: 1 n δ n δ k=0 σ k ζ , ω σ n δ -m ω 2 (n δ -k)! α ≥ 4 27 • 1 M + 2 .
div.p: Hence we get (55). This concludes the proof of Proposition 7.5.

div.head: Proof of Proposition 7.6
div.p: Consider M > 3, θ ∈ (0, 1), and δ ∈ (0, 1). Suppose that the event Ω m n δ occurs. Let ζ be a vector consisting of the roots F α,n δ inside D. Let ω be a vector consisting of the roots F α,n δ outside D. Consider i and j satisfying 0 ≤ i ≤ m and r α ≤ j ≤ m. From Proposition 6.7 it follows that for all 0
div.formula: ≤ l ≤ n δ -m σ n δ -l ω n δ n δ -l (n δ -l)! α/2 = (-1) n δ -l ξ n δ   (n δ -l)∧(rα-1) r=0 (-1) r w r • ξ l+r π α [l ; r] + η [n δ ] l   .
div.p: Therefore, for j ≤ k ≤ n δ -m + j and l = n δ -k (so that k -j = n δ -(l + j)) we have

Suppose we use: a M in conditions (ii) and (iii) in place of M ; b M in condition (iv) in place of M ; c M in condition (v) in place of M ; where a M , b M , and c M are some functions of M diverging to ∞ as M → ∞. Suppose we also modify the definitions of all the other events in Step 2 accordingly. Then we will get (93) with f (M, θ) = K 13 (D, m) b M C M + a m θ -1 , for some constant K 13 (D, m) > 0.

(i) Condition (i) defining Ω 3:1 ∞ δ ; θ ; M is same as condition (i) defining Ω 3:2 ∞ • ; θ ; M .

div.head: Appendix G: Glossary of Notations
div.p: Here we list some commonly used symbols with hyperlinks to their definitions.

figure.head: Notation 2 . 1 .
figure.figDesc: Consider m ∈ N. (a) Consider m-disjoint open balls B 1 , . . . , B m with rational centers and rational radii in D. Let Ψ(B 1 , . . . , B m ) := {Υ ∈ P(D) | |Υ ∩ B i | = 1 for all 1 ≤ i ≤ m} Then the countable collection A m in := {Ψ(B 1 , . . . , B m ) | B 1 , . . . , B m as above} is a countable basis of the Borel σ-algebra of point configurations on D with exactly m points.

Here 0 ∈ D m is the vector of all zeros. Observe that Γ ζ ′ ; ω bound Γ ζ ′ ; ω /Γ 0 ; ω it suffices to bound n-m j=1 ζ0-ωj ωj uniformly for ζ 0 ∈ D.

conditions w i = -σ i ζ for i = 0, . . . , m -1 and w i = 0 for i < 0. The eigenvalues of A are precisely the negatives of the inside zeros ζ 1 , . . . , ζ m . Due to the recursive structure in (66), w k is an element of A k applied to the vector -σ m-1 ζ , . . . , -σ 0 ζ . This implies w k is a linear combination of the entries of A k (with the coefficients in the linear combination being independent of k, but depending on D and m). If the eigenvalues of a matrix A have modulus < ρ ′ , then the entries of the matrix A k are o(ρ ′k ). Now the eigenvalues of A are ζ i 's and for each i we have |ζ i | ≤ r 0 . Therefore, for 1 ≤ r ≤ n we have |w r | ≤ (K 7 (D, m)) r for some constant K 7 (D, m) > 0. Switching variable to l = n -k in (65) we get

Next, we define the event Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M .
Definition 7.4 (The event Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M ). For M > 3, θ ∈ (0, 1), and δ ∈ (0, 1), let Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M be the event in which all of the following conditions are satisfied:
(i) Θ m n δ [θ] occurs ;
(ii) max 1≤s<sα I F α,n δ ; s ; 0, k(δ) ≤ M + 1 ;
(iii)
Next, we show that the event Ω 1:1 ∞ δ ; θ ; M is δ 3 -included in the event Ω 1:1 n δ δ ; θ ; M .

div.head: Appendix A: Reduction of D from a general domain to a disk
div.p: Here we show that in order to prove Theorems 3.1 and 3.2, it is enough to prove them when the domain D is a disk. First we will show this in the context of Theorem 3.1. The proof in the context of Theorem 3.2 is essentially the same. So we will only present a rough sketch and point out the main differences.

Let us suppose that Theorem 3.1 holds in the case D is a disk. Consider D to be a bounded open set in C whose boundary has zero Lebesgue measure. By the translation invariance of G ∞ , we assume that the origin is in the interior of D. Let D 0 be a disk centered at the origin containing the closure of D in its interior. By our assumption, Theorem 3.1 holds for D 0 . Thus, the conditional distribution of the vector consisting of points of G ∞ inside D 0 taken in uniform random order, given G ∞ ∩ D ∁ 0 = Υ 0 , is supported on D N0 0 . Moreover, this distribution has a density, say f 0 , with respect to the Lebesgue measure. This density satisfies

for some measurable functions m 0 , M 0 : P(D ∁ 0 ) → (0, ∞). Let ρ( • ; Υ out ) be the conditional distribution of the vector consisting of the points of G ∞ inside D taken in uniform random order, given G ∞,out = Υ. Then for all Borel subset A ⊂ D N we have ρ( A ; Υ ) = A f 0 ζ [1] , ζ [2] dL ζ [1] D N f 0 ζ [1] , ζ [2] dL ζ [1] , where ζ [2] is a vector consisting of the points of Υ in D 0 \ D, ζ [1] , ζ [2] denotes the concatenated vector, and the integrals are taken with respect to ζ [1] ∈ D N . The denominator is a measurable function of Υ.

To get an upper bound of ρ( A ; Υ ), we use the upper bound of f 0 from (114). The quantity M 0 (Υ 0 ) is measurable with respect to Υ because Υ 0 is the restriction of Υ on D ∁ 0 . We can split the Vandermonde term as △ ζ = △ ζ [1] • △ ζ [2] • Γ ζ [1] ; ζ [2] .

The term △ ζ [2] is measurable with respect to Υ. The term Γ ζ [1] ; ζ [2] is bounded above by (2r 0 ) N N0 where r 0 is the radius D 0 , and it is bounded below by θ N N0 where θ is the gap between points in Υ and the boundary of D. This gap is almost surely positive and measurable with respect to Υ. Hence we get that the ratio ρ( A ; Υ )/ A |△ ζ [1] | 2 dL(ζ [1] ) is bounded above and below by quantities measurable with respect to Υ. Then we get ( 11) by the Radon-Nikodym Theorem. Now let us show that in the context of Theorem 3.2 it is enough to assume that D is a disk. As mentioned before, we only point out the key differences. Suppose Theorem 3.2 is true when D is a disk. Now consider D to be a bounded open set in C whose boundary has zero Lebesgue measure. By the translation invariance of Z α,∞ , we take the origin to be in the interior of D. Let D 0 be a disk centered at origin containing D in its interior. Consider the event Z α,∞,out = Υ. Let Υ 0 := Υ ∩ D ∁ 0 ∈ P(D ∁ 0 ) be the restriction of Υ 0 on D ∁ 0 . By Theorem D.1, the point configuration Υ 0 determines the number N 0 , and the power sums up to order r α -1 s (0) = (s
rα-1 ) of points inside D 0 . Similarly, the point configuration Υ determines the number N , and the power sums up to order r α -1, say s = (s 1 , . . . , s rα-1 ) of the points inside D. Define

By the assumption that Theorem 3.2 is valid for D 0 we get that the conditional distribution of the vector of points inside D 0 , given Z α,∞ ∩ D ∁ 0 = Υ 0 , lives on Σ (0) , and has a density with respect to the Lebesgue measure on Σ (0) , say f 0 , which satisfies

for some measurable functions m 0 , M 0 : P(D ∁ 0 ) → (0, ∞). The rest of the proof is same as in the context of Theorem 3.1. The only difference is, instead D N we now have Σ, and instead of D N0 0 we have Σ (0) .
Appendix B: Ginibre Ensemble: Definition, Rigidity, Tolerance

Consider a n × n matrix M n whose entries are i.i.d. standard complex Gaussian random variables. The vector of its eigenvalues, in uniform random order, has a joint density with respect to the Lebesgue measure on C n given by

The set of eigenvalues of M n , considered as a random point configuration, is called the n-dimensional Ginibre ensemble. We denote it by G n . As n → ∞, G n converges in distribution to a point process called the (infinite) Ginibre ensemble, denoted by G ∞ . Both G n and G ∞ are determinantal point processes.

Recall that a determinantal point process on the Euclidean space R d with kernel K and background measure µ is a point process on R d whose k-point intensity functions with respect to the measure µ (k) are given by p k (x 1 , . . . , x k ) := det K(x i , x j ) 1≤i,j≤k .
The finite Ginibre ensemble G n is a determinantal point process with kernel K n (z, w) := n-1 k=0
with respect to the background measure dγ(z) = 1 π e -|z| 2 dL, where L denotes the Lebesgue measure on C. The Ginibre ensemble G ∞ is a determinantal point process with kernel

with respect to the measure γ. We can define (G n ) and G ∞ on the same probability space such that G n → G ∞ a.s. We refer to [30] for a brief explanation. The following rigidity and tolerance properties were established in [30].
